title: The Church: A Spiritual Calling
speaker: Elder Andrew Hoy
source_files: The Church_ A Spiritual Calling - Elder Andrew Hoy - 1 Peter 2_4-10.txt
outline: Coming to Christ as a living stone, chosen and precious (v.4)
Believers as living stones being built into a spiritual house (v.5)
The church as a chosen generation, royal priesthood, holy nation (v.9)
Called out of darkness into His marvelous light (v.9)
Formerly no people, now the people of God (v.10)
summary: An AM sermon on 1 Peter 2:4-10 examining the church as a spiritual calling. Elder Andrew Hoy expounds the corporate identity of God's people as living stones built into a spiritual house with Christ as the cornerstone. The church is called a chosen generation, royal priesthood, holy nation, and a people for God's own possession—called out of darkness into His marvelous light to proclaim His praises.
transcript:
We are certainly living in peculiar times. Many of us have had lots of conversations about what is going on in the world. My view tends to be that we are experiencing the ebbs and flows of history — there are certainly concerning things happening, especially in our culture, changes for the worse. But there have been other very difficult times to live in history. I think about what it would have been like to be a faithful Christian living in Nazi Germany, or someone trying to be faithful during the era of the French Revolution.

Amid all these conversations I have been having with people, there is a phrase I found myself using over and over again: hope in the church. I know that might strike you as a strange phrase. We know we are supposed to hope in God — and the church is full of people who are bound to let you down. The church has failed many people many times. But what I mean by it is this: the church is the only institution on earth that God has promised to uphold. Jesus said, "On this rock I will build my church, and the gates of hell will not prevail against it." Since the church is the one institution that will last, that is where we should be pouring our energy, our time, and our hope.

I believe this is something we see in the passage before us this morning. The Apostle Peter is writing to a suffering church — yes, the Peter who was so well known for sticking his foot in his mouth, who denied Jesus three times, who even after he was restored fell into a legalistic heresy. That Peter is now writing to Christians scattered throughout Asia Minor, trying to encourage them and help them suffer well. Among the encouragement he gives them are these words from 1 Peter 2:4–10, in which he roots their identity in their calling as the church.

As we turn to this passage, we will see that Peter shows us the church is a spiritual people with a spiritual calling. He explains five distinct traits that make the church the church, and tells us what the primary focus of the church should be.

## First Trait: The Church Is a People United to Christ Through Faith

The passage opens with the phrase "coming to him as to living stones." This is not a physical coming to Jesus — it is a spiritual coming. So what is the nature of that coming? There is only one right way to come to Christ, and that is through faith. Peter makes this explicit: verse 6 says, "he who believes on him will by no means be put to shame," and verse 7 says, "to you who believe he is precious."

So the church is a people. I think we know this — the church is the people, not the building and not merely our gatherings, though those are real expressions of it. First and foremost, when Scripture speaks of the church, it speaks of the people of God.

And this passage gives us helpful insight into the nature of true, saving belief. First, true faith identifies personally with Christ. In verses 4 and 5, Christ is the cornerstone and we are the living stones — we are personally united to him in what he is doing. Second, true faith clings to him for hope: "he who believes on him will by no means be put to shame" — there is a certain desperation to our faith in which we cling to him as our only source of rescue. Third, true faith views Christ as precious: true belief sees Christ as greater than any treasure this world has to offer.

None of us believe perfectly. But we should always be striving to grow in faith, to have it more conformed to the scriptural image, and to cry out like the man who said, "I believe — help my unbelief."

Now, verses 6 through 8 develop the idea of being united to Christ using a series of Old Testament quotations. Christ is the chief cornerstone — the first stone laid, upon which everything else is aligned. We are the stones united to that cornerstone. These verses also show that as Christ was rejected by men, he was chosen by God. And so for those of us who are personally identified with Christ — tied to him by faith — we will also be rejected by men. But as we are rejected by men, in our being tied to Christ, we are chosen by God. As we consider the glory of Christ in his resurrection and the treasures tied with that, we find strength to endure hardship.

Suffering should not come as a surprise to Christians. Consider the Christ we worship. When the world seems completely opposed to us, we should not be startled or afraid, but recognize this is the nature of the world we live in — and take hope, for just as Christ overcame the world, so will we as long as we cling to him.

## Second Trait: The Church Is a People Chosen by God

In verse 9, Peter says the church is "a chosen generation." This idea of a chosen people points to the scriptural teaching on predestination. Whenever the identity of the church is discussed at length in Scripture, nearby you can find some sort of teaching on predestination — Ephesians 1 and 2, Romans 9, and here in 1 Peter.

There are really two basic views on election. One is that God chose us based on foreseeing faith — that in eternity past God looked forward at creation to find those who would say "I believe," and then chose them. The other view is that God chose some for his own reasons, not based on foreseeing faith, but out of his infinite, eternal, and holy wisdom. And implied in that is that there are some he did not choose. It is this latter view that I believe Scripture teaches and that our church confessionally holds.

Turn with me briefly to Ephesians 1:3–6:

> *"Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who has blessed us with every spiritual blessing in the heavenly places in Christ, just as He chose us in Him before the foundation of the world, that we should be holy and without blame before Him in love, having predestined us to adoption as sons by Jesus Christ to Himself, according to the good pleasure of His will, to the praise of the glory of His grace, by which He made us accepted in the Beloved."*

Not according to foreseeing faith, but according to the good pleasure of his will. And for those who would argue that election is based on foreseeing faith, they will have to reckon with Ephesians 2:8, which demonstrates that even faith is a gift from God.

Now, predestination is a difficult doctrine, and it is natural to struggle with it. If you have enjoyed the preaching at this church but are not sure what to make of this teaching, you are not alone. I would even say that if you have never really struggled with this doctrine at any level, perhaps you have not fully considered the weight of it.

In our passage, verse 8 helps us with one of the chief difficulties of election. The question is: if God is in complete control over all things, how are we still responsible? How can someone who sins under the complete sovereignty of God be punished for sins committed under the complete sovereignty of God?

Look at verse 8: "They stumble, being disobedient to the word to which they also were appointed." These people are fully responsible for their actions, and yet God was fully in control of them. Scripture continuously upholds both of these realities. We do not get a full philosophical reconciliation — Scripture simply holds them both up together.

That is a difficult thing, but also a precious thing. To realize that from eternity past, in his infinite wisdom, God chose some to redeem and save — and that there is no other being in the universe equipped to make that decision — should be a source of great comfort. Do we really want that ultimate decision left in the hands of humans?

And this doctrine is deeply practical when we think about the church. I have many wonderful Arminian brothers and sisters in Christ, and Arminians — those who hold that election is based on foreseeing faith — tend to see the church as a voluntary association: individual Christians who happen to gather once a week to be fed by the Word of God. But those who hold to God's sovereign election tend to see the church as a people God is sovereignly working to bring together for his good purposes. It is not a coincidence that the people sitting next to you, behind you, and in front of you were brought together. God has ordained each person here to be among us. We will be spending eternity together as his people.

## Third Trait: The Church Is a People United Together as One People of God

We see this in verse 5 — living stones being built up into a spiritual house — in verse 9 — a holy nation — and in verse 10: "who once were not a people, but are now the people of God."

This passage demonstrates that there is one people of God. Some theologies teach that there are two peoples of God — Israel and the church — but this passage shows there is only one. All the imagery in this passage points to Israel: living stones, holy priesthood, chosen generation, royal priesthood, holy nation, his own special people — each of these is a direct Old Testament allusion to Israel, now being used to describe the church. And verse 10, "who once were not a people but are now the people of God," echoes Hosea.

Understanding the relationship between Israel and the church transforms how we view the church and our place in it. The church is not a place where individual saved people happen to gather. We are being saved into the people of God. It is not inappropriate to say that we are being saved into the church.

Look around you — these are the people God has knit you together with in his sovereignty. He has saved you into this specific body here in Olympia, and into his church universal. We are united not only with Christians around the world but with Christians throughout history — including all the faithful of ancient Israel. We are part of something far bigger than just us. That should be a source of great encouragement and hope.

## Fourth Trait: The Church Is a People Created to Worship God

We see this in verses 5 and 9. Verse 5: "you also, as living stones, are being built up a spiritual house, a holy priesthood to offer up spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God through Jesus Christ." Verse 9: "that you may proclaim the praises of him who called you out of darkness into his marvelous light."

Peter calls us priests. A priest is one who brings the people of God before God. A prophet brings the word of God to the people; a priest brings the people to God. And Peter says we are all priests — this is what we call the priesthood of believers. We all have direct access to God through Christ. Pastors and elders are not mediating priests standing between us and God.

But priests primarily offer sacrifices. Now, the bloody sacrifices of the Old Testament pointed to the ultimate propitiatory sacrifice of Christ — the wrath-appeasing sacrifice that makes us righteous in the sight of God. There is nothing we can personally bring to accomplish that; Christ alone did it as the great high priest. But we do bring sacrifices of thanksgiving and praise. And what is the nature of those sacrifices? I believe they are ultimately sacrifices of obedience. Romans 12 puts it this way: "present your bodies as a living sacrifice, holy and acceptable to God, which is your spiritual worship."

Now when we think of God taking delight in our obedience, some may picture a tyrant who takes a sick pleasure in people just doing what he says — a power trip or a grumpy father who demands compliance. That is not the picture Scripture gives us. Peter quotes from chapter 1: "Be holy, for I am holy." Our calls to obedience are calls to be like God. And God takes great delight in us being like him.

Here is an image that came to me as I was preparing this sermon. I was cleaning out my work van, and my youngest son Edmund was outside with me. After a little while I realized he had started bringing things from the garage to my van — which was actually a little counterproductive, since I was mostly taking things from the van to the garage. But there was a part of what Edmund was doing that created great delight in my heart. He saw me going back and forth and he wanted to join in the work. He was trying to be like me. And I believe that when we seek to obey God from a heart of faith, the delight God gets from that is because we are being like him — and that is ultimately what he desires.

One of our greatest expressions of this obedience is gathering with the saints weekly for corporate worship. We are commanded to assemble, and God tells us how to worship him — in faith, with a pure heart, according to the dictates of his Word. Let us not take lightly what we do here weekly. We come together to express worship by praying to him, singing to him, listening to him, and being together as his people. It is a great honor and privilege to do so.

## Fifth Trait: The Church Is a People Called to Display God's Glory

In verse 5, we are referred to as living stones — an allusion to the temple. Verse 9 says explicitly that we exist "that you may proclaim the praises of him who called you out of darkness into his marvelous light." And verse 12, just outside our passage, continues: "by your good works, which they observe, they may glorify God in the day of visitation."

The temple was the special dwelling place of God's presence. In the old covenant, God's presence was made known through the tabernacle and then the temple. Now, as living stones built into God's spiritual temple, God's presence is made known through the church. God dwells among us in a special way, displaying himself to the nations.

Where do people go to find the God of Scripture? They do not go to a building in Mecca or a seat of learning or a stock market. They turn to his people. We have a high and holy calling as his church.

And I want you to take special note of the corporate nature of our witness in this passage: stones being built up into a spiritual house. Our corporate identity as the church is central to our witness in the world. We cannot neglect our obligations to one another. Yes, this means attending weekly worship, but it means much more.

Look around at the people next to you. These are not only people God has redeemed and called together in his divine providence — these are people he has called you to be committed to, to serve, and to love. Do you know the people of this church? Do you love the people of this church? It is in our love for one another that we serve as a witness to the world of the great and merciful acts of God.

## Conclusion

People of the church, receive the Lord's call today to take seriously your identity and your calling as a church. You are united to Christ by faith. You are chosen by God. You are part of the family of God, one people throughout history and around the world. You are called to worship him and called to display his glory.

May your identity as the church be an encouragement to you, and may you, by God's grace, take heed of your calling as the church today.
